canonical_summary:
A vehicle operator license, commonly called a VOL, is the single authorization that the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) now issues for U.S. commercial launch and reentry under 14 CFR Part 450. The rule consolidated four legacy regimes (Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435) into one performance-based framework when it became effective on March 10, 2021, replacing the older bifurcation between launch-specific and launch operator licenses with one portfolio license that can cover multiple flights, multiple vehicle configurations within a family, and multiple sites.

Section 450.3 sets the scope of the license, Section 450.5 conditions issuance on completion of all required approvals (policy, payload, safety, and environmental review), and Section 450.7 fixes a maximum term of five years from the issuance date. Within that envelope, a licensee may fly an unlimited number of qualifying missions, modify the license to add new sites, configurations, or splashdown areas, and renew before expiration.

The five-year transition window for legacy licenses closed on March 9, 2026. As of March 18, 2026, the FAA confirmed no U.S. commercial operator remained under legacy authority. Part 450 is now the exclusive licensing pathway for orbital and suborbital, expendable and reusable, launch and reentry operations in the United States.

faqs.question: What is an FAA vehicle operator license?
faqs.answer: A vehicle operator license, or VOL, is the authorization the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation issues under 14 CFR Part 450 to allow a U.S. commercial operator to conduct launches or reentries. It is a single, portfolio-style license that covers one or more missions using the same vehicle or family of vehicles. Issuance requires completion of policy, payload, safety, and environmental review under Subparts B and C of Part 450. The license replaces the legacy bifurcation between launch-specific licenses and launch operator licenses that had existed under former Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435.

faqs.question: How long is a Part 450 launch operator license valid?
faqs.answer: Under 14 CFR Section 450.7, a vehicle operator license is valid for the period the Administrator determines is necessary to conduct the licensed activity, but it may not exceed five years from the issuance date. The Administrator sets the actual term case by case based on the nature of the licensed activity, so a sounding-rocket program and an orbital reusable launch vehicle program may receive different terms within that ceiling. Within the term, the operator can fly an unlimited number of qualifying launches or reentries, and must renew or apply for a new license before expiration to continue operating.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a launch-specific license and a launch operator license?
faqs.answer: The distinction was codified in former 14 CFR Section 415.3, added by Amendment 415-03 at 64 FR 19586 on April 21, 1999. A launch-specific license authorized one or more launches with the same parameters of one vehicle type from one site, and identified each launch by name or mission. A launch operator license authorized launches from one site within a parameter envelope of vehicles from the same family, for a five-year term. Part 450 collapsed both into a single vehicle operator license effective March 10, 2021, with mission specificity handled inside the license rather than as a separate license type.

faqs.question: When did Part 450 take effect, and when did the legacy regime end?
faqs.answer: The FAA published the Streamlined Launch and Reentry License Requirements final rule at 85 FR 79566 on December 10, 2020. Part 450 became effective on March 10, 2021, opening a five-year transition window during which legacy licenses issued under Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 could continue to operate. That window closed on March 9, 2026. On March 18, 2026, the FAA confirmed that every U.S. commercial launch and reentry operator had either converted to Part 450 or let its legacy license expire, making Part 450 the exclusive licensing pathway.

faqs.question: Which operators hold Part 450 vehicle operator licenses?
faqs.answer: By the time the legacy window closed, the FAA reported issuing fourteen Part 450 licenses. Public records and FAA announcements identify SpaceX Starship/Super Heavy (VOL 23-129, issued April 14, 2023), the SpaceX Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon portfolio (transitioned ahead of the 2026 deadline), Blue Origin New Glenn (issued December 27, 2024), Blue Origin New Shepard (transitioned from LRLO 17-105), Rocket Lab Electron (transitioned from LLO 20-120), United Launch Alliance Vulcan and Atlas, Firefly Aerospace Alpha, and Astra, which received the first Part 450 license in 2022.

faqs.question: What scope can a single vehicle operator license cover?
faqs.answer: Section 450.3 authorizes a licensee to conduct one or more launches or reentries using the same vehicle or family of vehicles. In practice the FAA has interpreted that broadly. A single VOL may cover multiple vehicle configurations within a family, multiple U.S. and non-U.S. launch or reentry sites, varied trajectory and splashdown envelopes, and recovery operations including landings, catches, and post-landing safing. The scope is the envelope the Administrator approves at issuance based on the operator's application package, and operators can later modify the license to expand that envelope without restarting the entire review.

faqs.question: How long does FAA review of a Part 450 application take?
faqs.answer: The statutory clock is 180 days under 51 U.S.C. Section 50904 once the FAA accepts an application as sufficiently complete. The practical timeline is longer because the agency commonly requests additional information or pauses the clock pending means-of-compliance acceptances under Section 450.35. The FAA recommends pre-application consultation under Section 450.31 well before formal submission, often six to twelve months in advance, to scope novel hazards and align means of compliance. Operators with new vehicles or unusual mission profiles should plan for 12 to 24 months total from first engagement to license issuance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a VOL, a launch operator license, and a launch site operator license?
faqs.answer: A vehicle operator license under Part 450 authorizes a company to conduct launch or reentry operations with a particular vehicle. A launch operator license is the legacy term for the same kind of vehicle-level authorization under former Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435; the term still appears in older license numbers like LLO 19-110 for Falcon or LRLO 17-105 for New Shepard. A launch site operator license, governed by 14 CFR Part 420, authorizes a spaceport operator to offer a site to launch customers and is a separate instrument that does not authorize flight.

faqs.question: What is the SpaceX Starship VOL 23-129, and how has it changed?
faqs.answer: VOL 23-129 is the Part 450 vehicle operator license the FAA issued to SpaceX on April 14, 2023 for Starship/Super Heavy operations from Boca Chica, Texas, signed by Michelle S. Murray. Its five-year term runs to April 14, 2028. The original was limited to the first integrated flight test. The FAA has since issued multiple revisions, including Rev 2 through at least Rev 6, that removed the first-flight-only condition, added splashdown areas in the Pacific and near Australia, and authorized catch operations at the launch tower. Each revision required environmental and safety review.

faqs.question: When does an operator need to modify a Part 450 license?
faqs.answer: Modifications are required when an operator wants to fly outside the envelope the FAA approved at issuance. Common triggers include adding a new launch or reentry site, adding a new vehicle configuration within or outside the approved family, expanding trajectory or splashdown envelopes, adding new payload classes, and adding recovery activities such as booster catches or droneship landings. Smaller changes, such as routine updates to the flight safety analysis baseline that remain inside the approved means of compliance, may be handled through reporting and continuing-accuracy requirements without a formal license modification.

faqs.question: What does FAA pre-application consultation involve under Part 450?
faqs.answer: Section 450.31 directs operators to engage AST before filing a complete application. In practice the consultation runs through multiple meetings in which the operator describes the vehicle, mission concepts, hazards, and proposed means of compliance for each performance requirement. AST identifies novel aspects, surfaces likely safety questions, and either accepts the operator's proposed means of compliance under Section 450.35 or asks for revisions. The agency strongly recommends that pre-application consultation begin well before formal submission. AST has stated that pre-application engagement is the single most important predictor of a smooth review and a timely license issuance.

faqs.question: Does a vehicle operator license cover financial responsibility and third-party liability?
faqs.answer: Financial responsibility is governed by 14 CFR Part 440, which Part 450 Subpart F incorporates. The FAA conducts a Maximum Probable Loss (MPL) analysis for each licensed operation and requires the operator to carry liability insurance, or demonstrate equivalent financial responsibility, up to the MPL amount or the statutory cap of $500 million for third-party claims, whichever is lower. A separate amount covers U.S. government property. For example, the New Shepard LRLO 17-105 Rev 4 set MPL at $150 million. MPL determinations are revisited at license issuance, modification, and renewal.

h1: FAA Vehicle Operator License Under 14 CFR Part 450
meta_title: FAA Vehicle Operator License (Part 450): Full Guide
meta_description: Complete reference to the FAA Part 450 vehicle operator license: 14 CFR 450.3, 450.5, 450.7 scope and duration, legacy conversion, issued VOLs, and review path.

sections.heading: What a vehicle operator license is, and what 14 CFR Section 450.7 says
paragraphs: A vehicle operator license, abbreviated VOL, is the single instrument the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) issues under 14 CFR Part 450 to authorize a U.S. commercial operator to conduct launches or reentries. Part 450 became effective March 10, 2021, replacing the prior bifurcation between launch-specific and launch operator licenses under former Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435.

Three sections in Subpart A define the instrument. Section 450.3 sets the scope: a vehicle operator license authorizes a licensee to conduct one or more launches or reentries using the same vehicle or family of vehicles, from one or more sites, within the envelope approved by the Administrator. Section 450.5 conditions issuance on the applicant having obtained all approvals required under Part 450, including the policy and payload reviews in Subpart B, the safety review in Subpart C, and the environmental review under the National Environmental Policy Act.

Section 450.7 fixes the temporal envelope. It provides that a vehicle operator license is valid for the period the Administrator determines is necessary to conduct the licensed activity, but may not exceed five years from the issuance date. The Administrator sets the actual term case by case based on the nature of the licensed activity. Within that envelope, the licensee may conduct an unlimited number of qualifying launches or reentries, which is the practical engine of the multi-mission model that Part 450 was designed to enable.

subsections.heading: The three operative sections at a glance
bullets: Section 450.3: scope, one or more launches or reentries, same vehicle or family of vehicles, multi-site permitted
Section 450.5: issuance conditioned on policy, payload, safety, and environmental approvals
Section 450.7: maximum five-year term, actual term set by the Administrator

sections.heading: Launch-specific licenses vs. launch operator licenses: the legacy history
paragraphs: The U.S. commercial launch licensing regime originates in the Commercial Space Launch Act of 1984, Public Law 98-575, codified at 51 U.S.C. Chapter 509. The Office of Commercial Space Transportation issued its first commercial launch regulations in April 1988 and was transferred into the FAA as AST in November 1995. The bifurcation between launch-specific and launch operator licenses was codified by Amendment 415-03, published at 64 FR 19586 on April 21, 1999, with the operative text at 64 FR 19616.

Former 14 CFR Section 415.3(a) authorized a launch-specific license for one or more launches with the same launch parameters of one vehicle type from one site, identifying each launch by name or mission. Section 415.3(b) authorized a launch operator license for launches from one site within a parameter envelope of vehicles from the same family, valid for five years from issuance. The 1999 rulemaking extended the operator license duration from a two-year administrative norm to five years and gave both categories regulatory text. Companion frameworks for reusable launch vehicles and reentry vehicles followed in 2000 at 65 FR 56618 (Parts 431 and 435), and Part 417 was added in 2006 at 71 FR 50508 to consolidate ELV launch safety requirements.

The Streamlined Launch and Reentry License Requirements final rule, published at 85 FR 79566 on December 10, 2020, collapsed all four legacy regimes into Part 450 and unified the license types into a single vehicle operator license. Legacy licenses were preserved through a five-year transition window that closed March 9, 2026.

sections.heading: Vehicle operator licenses issued since 2021, per operator
paragraphs: By the end of the five-year transition, the FAA reported issuing fourteen Part 450 licenses. The FAA license database at faa.gov/space/licenses is the authoritative ledger. The most consequential issuances and conversions are summarized below.

subsections.heading: SpaceX Starship/Super Heavy: VOL 23-129
paragraphs: VOL 23-129 was issued April 14, 2023 by AST signatory Michelle S. Murray, authorizing SpaceX to conduct launches of Starship/Super Heavy from the Boca Chica (Starbase) Launch Complex in Cameron County, Texas to splashdown areas in the Gulf of Mexico and Pacific Ocean. The license runs five years to April 14, 2028.

The original issuance was limited to the first integrated flight test. The FAA has since posted Rev 2 and additional revisions through Rev 5.4 and Rev 6 that removed the first-flight-only condition, expanded splashdown zones (including Indian Ocean trajectories near Australia), and added catch operations at the launch tower. License conditions require an FAA Safety Inspector on site at Boca Chica during pre-flight operations.

subsections.heading: SpaceX Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon
paragraphs: Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy operated for years under legacy license LLO 19-110, originally issued February 15, 2019, with Rev 16 posted September 1, 2023. SpaceX completed the Part 450 transition for the Falcon portfolio and the Dragon reentry segment ahead of the March 9, 2026 deadline, per the FAA's March 17, 2026 announcement. The resulting VOL covers multiple Falcon configurations across SLC-40, LC-39A, and SLC-4E under a single performance-based authorization.

subsections.heading: Blue Origin New Shepard and New Glenn
paragraphs: New Shepard operates under the LRLO 17-105 family, originally issued in 2017 and renewed as Rev 4 on August 13, 2023 with a two-year term and Maximum Probable Loss of $150 million. Blue Origin completed its New Shepard Part 450 transition before the deadline. Separately, New Glenn received its own Part 450 VOL on December 27, 2024 with a five-year term, the first Part 450 license issued to Blue Origin.

subsections.heading: Rocket Lab Electron
paragraphs: Electron's U.S. authorization is the LLO 20-120 family, covering launches from Launch Complex 2 at the Mid-Atlantic Regional Spaceport on Wallops Island, Virginia. Rev 4 was digitally signed March 15, 2024 by Katie Lynne Cranor. Rocket Lab completed the Part 450 transition ahead of the March 9, 2026 deadline.

subsections.heading: Other operators
paragraphs: Astra received the first Part 450 license in 2022. Firefly Aerospace (Alpha), United Launch Alliance (Atlas and Vulcan), and additional operators converted through 2024 and 2025. Stoke Space's Nova vehicle remains in the Part 450 pre-application consultation pipeline as of the FAA's March 17, 2026 statement; Stoke secured the SLC-14 site-development package in October 2024 but had not yet received a Part 450 vehicle license at that time.

sections.heading: Converting from legacy Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 to Part 450
paragraphs: The transition provisions in the final rule at 85 FR 79566 preserved every legacy license already in force on March 10, 2021 but capped their lifespan at five years. Operators had three choices: continue operating the unchanged legacy license until it expired, voluntarily come into compliance with Part 450 at any time before March 10, 2026, or apply for a new Part 450 license to cover expanded operations.

The conversion is not so much an amendment as a replacement. Operators initiate pre-application consultation under Section 450.31, submit means-of-compliance proposals under Section 450.35 for each Part 450 performance requirement, file a complete application, work the FAA review (statutory 180-day clock under 51 U.S.C. Section 50904, with pauses common), and surrender the predecessor legacy license once the new Part 450 VOL issues. Several operators ran parallel legacy and Part 450 licenses for short periods to bridge specific missions.
By March 18, 2026, the FAA confirmed that no U.S. launch operator remained under legacy authority. Part 450 is now the exclusive licensing pathway for U.S. commercial launch and reentry operations.

subsections.heading: The five-step conversion path
bullets: Pre-application consultation under Section 450.31, often six to twelve months in advance
Means-of-compliance submissions under Section 450.35 for each performance requirement
Complete application submission and FAA acceptance, which starts the 180-day clock
Issuance of the new Part 450 VOL and surrender of the legacy license
Continuing operations under Part 450 Subparts D and E for reporting, modification, and renewal

sections.heading: What scope a vehicle operator license can cover
paragraphs: Section 450.3 expressly contemplates a portfolio-scale license. The phrase one or more launches or reentries permits serial use of a single license across an unlimited number of flights during the license term. The phrase same vehicle or family of vehicles permits different configurations within an approved family without returning to the FAA for a new license, provided variations remain within the approved package. Paragraphs (b) and (c) define launch and reentry by activity bookends rather than discrete missions, which allows those bookends to repeat as often as the licensee wishes during the term.

In practice, a single VOL may cover multiple launch or reentry sites (including U.S. and non-U.S. sites), multiple trajectory and splashdown envelopes, and recovery activities including landings, catches, droneship recoveries, and post-landing safing. The actual scope is the envelope the Administrator approves at issuance based on the operator's application package. Operators have used VOL 23-129 to fly Starship across multiple expanding splashdown zones, and the new Falcon VOL covers Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy configurations across three sites at SLC-40, LC-39A, and SLC-4E.

subsections.heading: Elements a Part 450 VOL can encompass
bullets: Multiple launches and reentries during a single five-year term
Multiple vehicle configurations within an approved family
Multiple U.S. and non-U.S. launch or reentry sites
Multiple trajectory profiles, orbits, and splashdown zones
Recovery activities, including booster landings and catches
Varied payload classes within the approved policy and payload review

sections.heading: Common reasons to expand or modify a vehicle operator license
paragraphs: Modifications are the routine, expected mechanism for keeping a VOL current with an operator's program. Because Part 450 issues a portfolio license up front, modifications expand or refine the envelope rather than restart the entire review. The FAA's Subpart D and E mechanics govern the process. The Starship VOL 23-129 has been modified several times since April 2023, illustrating each of the common drivers below.

Modifications still require FAA review proportionate to the change. Adding a launch site triggers a new policy review and environmental analysis. Adding a vehicle configuration triggers safety review against the proposed means of compliance. The standard rule of thumb is that any change outside the approved envelope is a modification; routine updates that remain within the approved means of compliance are handled through reporting and continuing-accuracy requirements without a formal license action.

subsections.heading: Frequent modification triggers
bullets: Adding a new launch or reentry site
Adding a new vehicle configuration within or outside the approved family
Expanding trajectory or splashdown envelopes (Starship Pacific and Indian Ocean expansions)
Adding recovery operations such as booster catches at the launch tower
Adding payload classes that fall outside the approved policy and payload review
Updating the flight safety analysis baseline beyond the approved means of compliance
Renewing or extending the license term within the five-year cap of Section 450.7

sections.heading: The FAA review path
paragraphs: The FAA review path for a Part 450 vehicle operator license has four major checkpoints: pre-application consultation, means-of-compliance acceptance, formal application review, and post-issuance compliance. AST treats early engagement as the single most important predictor of a smooth review, and recommends pre-application consultation begin six to twelve months before formal submission for established vehicle classes and longer for novel ones.

The statutory clock is 180 days under 51 U.S.C. Section 50904 once AST accepts the application as sufficiently complete. The practical clock is longer because the agency frequently requests additional information or pauses pending means-of-compliance resolution under Section 450.35. AST also coordinates environmental review under NEPA, which can run on a parallel track and is often the gating activity for new sites or vehicle classes.

After issuance, the operator continues under Subparts D and E, which set out reporting, modification, renewal, and continuing-accuracy obligations. Financial responsibility is governed by 14 CFR Part 440, which Part 450 Subpart F incorporates by reference; the FAA conducts a Maximum Probable Loss analysis at issuance and revisits it at modification and renewal. The FAA chartered the Launch and Reentry License Requirements Improvement Aerospace Rulemaking Committee (the Part 450 SpARC) in late 2024 to deliver recommendations on flight safety analyses, system safety, and means of compliance, feeding the next round of rulemaking.

subsections.heading: Key review checkpoints
bullets: Pre-application consultation under Section 450.31, scoping novel hazards and means-of-compliance approach
Means-of-compliance submissions under Section 450.35 for each performance requirement
Application acceptance and 180-day statutory review under 51 U.S.C. Section 50904
Policy, payload, safety, and environmental review under Part 450 Subparts B and C
Maximum Probable Loss analysis and financial responsibility under Part 440
Issuance under Section 450.5 and continuing operations under Subparts D and E
